questions.question: List and explain the six requirements for a public key cryptosystem as defined by Diffie and Hellman.
keyPoints: R1: Computationally easy for any party to generate a public-private key pair
R2: Computationally easy for a sender to encrypt using the recipient's public key
R3: Computationally easy for the receiver to decrypt using their private key
R4: Computationally infeasible to determine the private key from the public key
R5: Computationally infeasible to recover plaintext from ciphertext and public key alone
R6 (optional): Either key can be used for encryption with the other for decryption, enabling digital signatures

questions.question: Define and differentiate between a one-way function and a one-way trapdoor function. Why is the trapdoor essential for public key cryptography?
keyPoints: One-way function: easy to compute Y=f(X) but infeasible to reverse X from Y
One-way trapdoor function: same as one-way, but reverse becomes easy with secret trapdoor information
The trapdoor is essential because without it, even the legitimate key holder could not decrypt messages
Example: RSA uses factoring as one-way; knowing p,q (trapdoor) makes computing d easy

questions.question: Explain how the requirements for public key cryptography are satisfied by the RSA algorithm.
keyPoints: R1: Key generation involves choosing random primes and computing modular inverse - efficient algorithms exist
R2: Encryption C=M^e mod n uses fast modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply)
R3: Decryption M=C^d mod n is similarly efficient using the same algorithm
R4: Deriving d from (e,n) requires factoring n, which is computationally infeasible for large n
R5: Recovering M from C requires the private key d; without factoring n, this is infeasible
R6: RSA supports both encryption and signing because the operations are symmetric in e and d

questions.question: What are the three main computationally hard problems used as the foundation for public key cryptosystems? Name an algorithm based on each.
keyPoints: Integer Factorization Problem (IFP): basis for RSA
Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP): basis for Diffie-Hellman and ElGamal
Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP): basis for ECC
All three are believed to be computationally hard with no known polynomial-time classical algorithms
